1: The prophets Haggai and Zechariah begin their ministries — the key intervention that ends the sixteen-year building halt. Haggai’s oracle in Haggai 1:2-11 confronts the people’s rationalizations about why the time is not right to build; Zechariah’s visions (Zech 1-8) provide cosmic reassurance and promise. They prophesy “in the name of the God of Israel who is over them” — in contrast to the letter-writing opponents who spoke in the name of imperial administrative authority, the prophets speak in the name of YHWH himself.
2: Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Jeshua son of Jozadak — the civil and religious leaders — rise up and resume building “with the support of the prophets of God who were with them.” The double leadership structure (Davidic governor + Zadokite high priest) supported by prophetic encouragement is the post-exilic covenant community’s organizational form. Haggai 1:14 records that “the LORD stirred up the spirit of Zerubbabel” — the resumption is divinely initiated.
3: Tattenai the governor of Trans-Euphrates and Shetharboznai arrive to investigate: “Who authorized you to rebuild this house and to complete these walls?” Tattenai’s inquiry is a formal administrative investigation, not hostile suppression. The contrast with Rehum and Shimshai is marked: Tattenai will conduct a fair process, report accurately to Darius, and implement his decision obediently (Ezra 6:13). He is an honest imperial official rather than a politically motivated opponent.
4: “We also asked them, ‘What are the names of the men carrying out this building?’” The recording of names serves both a threatening function (official registration for potential punishment) and a protective one (the names become official records that Darius can verify). In the event, the naming is protective: the builders’ identities are on record as subject to Darius’s investigation, preventing arbitrary suppression while the matter is under royal review.
5: “The eye of their God (ʿên ʾĕlāhᵉhôn) was on the elders of the Jews, so the officials could not stop them.” The divine watchfulness — expressed through the singular “eye” — creates a window of continuing work. The elders are not stopped while the report goes to Darius because YHWH’s attention to their situation effectively suspends the normal administrative process. This is the Chronicler’s theological statement on providential protection: the covenant community does not cease its work merely because an investigation is underway.
6: The letter Tattenai and Shetharboznai send to Darius is reproduced in the Aramaic source (Ezra 5:7-17). Unlike Rehum’s accusatory letter, Tattenai’s is genuinely investigative: he reports what he found, includes the builders’ own account, and asks the king to search the archives. This is administrative fairness: the letter lets the Judean builders speak for themselves and invites royal verification rather than royal suppression.
7: The formal Aramaic letter opening — “To King Darius, all greetings (šᵉlāmāh kōlāʾ).” The standard Persian epistolary formula; greetings and health wished to the king before the substance begins. The formal opening signals this is official correspondence, not private communication.
8: “We went to the province of Judah, to the house of the great God.” Tattenai identifies the building as the house of “the great God” (ʾĕlāhāʾ rabbāʾ) — an honorific Persian officials would use for YHWH, paralleling how they spoke of Ahura Mazda. The description of the building work as using large stones and timbers is archaeologically consistent with descriptions of Herodian and Persian-era masonry.
9: The question about authorization — “Who authorized you to build this house and to complete these walls?” — is exactly the question the opponents were hoping would produce a “no one authorized this” answer leading to suppression. Instead, the builders’ answer (the Cyrus decree) will vindicate them through the same archive system that Rehum used against them.
10: “We also asked for their names, so that we could record the names of their leaders and inform you.” The administrative scrupulousness of Tattenai’s investigation is complete: he records everything — the state of the building, the builders’ self-description, their names, their claim of royal authorization. The transparency of his report is what makes the case favorable to the builders when Darius reviews it.
11: The elders’ response to Tattenai begins with theological self-identification: “We are servants of the God of heaven and earth.” The universalist title — God of heaven and earth, not merely the God of Israel — is appropriate for a Persian context (cf. Cyrus’s decree, Ezra 1:2). The elders know their audience; they frame YHWH in terms the Persian official can recognize as referring to the supreme deity.
12: “But because our ancestors provoked the God of heaven to anger, he handed them over to Nebuchadnezzar.” The remarkable confession before the Persian official: the elders attribute the Babylonian exile not to Babylonian military superiority but to divine judgment for their ancestors’ sin. This is the prophetic interpretation of the exile (Jer 25; Dan 9; Neh 9) stated plainly to a pagan administrator. The theological honesty of the elders’ response is striking: they do not blame the Babylonians; they own the cause.
13: The Cyrus decree cited: “In the first year of Cyrus king of Babylon, Cyrus the king issued a decree to rebuild this house of God.” The title “king of Babylon” (rather than king of Persia) is Cyrus’s legitimate Babylonian regnal title — he used it after conquering Babylon in 539 BCE. The elders cite the most legally compelling form of the decree: the king of Babylon (the kingdom that destroyed the temple) authorizes its rebuilding.
14: The temple vessels are mentioned — Cyrus gave them to Sheshbazzar (= Zerubbabel, or his predecessor as governor) for return to Jerusalem. The return of the vessels is the physical evidence of the Cyrus decree’s authenticity: if the vessels were returned, the decree was implemented. The vessels’ presence in Jerusalem is material evidence supporting the builders’ account.
15: “Cyrus told him, ‘Take these vessels, bring them to the temple in Jerusalem, and let God’s house be rebuilt where it was before.’” The builders’ account of the Cyrus decree — remembered and transmitted over two decades — matches the decree as found at Ecbatana (Ezra 6:3-5). The consistency between the builders’ oral tradition and the written archival record is the crucial verification.
16: Sheshbazzar laid the foundation of God’s house in Jerusalem, and from that time to the present it has been under construction without being completed. The elders honestly admit the incompleteness: the foundation was laid in year 1 of Cyrus (536 BCE) and the building is still unfinished in year 2 of Darius (520 BCE) — sixteen years of interruption. The candor serves them: they are not hiding the building halt, and they have a legitimate explanation for it (opposition, as documented).
17: The elders ask Tattenai to request a royal archive search: “Verify whether Cyrus the king issued a decree to rebuild God’s house in Jerusalem.” The elders’ confidence in the archive search is itself evidence of their good faith: they are calling for the very investigation that will vindicate them. They stake their entire case on the existence of a documentary record they may never have seen themselves but believe exists.
